questions.text: What is the fundamental difference between a "technical Single Point of Failure" and "governance censorship" in centralised systems?

options.text: Technical SPOFs affect large companies; censorship only affects individual users
Technical SPOFs are more dangerous than censorship because they affect more users simultaneously
Technical SPOFs are accidental infrastructure failures; censorship is a deliberate governance decision to restrict access - but both result from a single entity having central control
Technical SPOFs can be fixed by the platform; censorship cannot be fixed because it is government-mandated

questions.explanation: Explanation: The distinction is critical for both understanding the problem and designing solutions. Technical SPOFs (like the AWS December 2021 outage) are unintentional - infrastructure engineers try to prevent them but cannot guarantee zero failures. Governance censorship (like Instagram suppressing a journalist's reach) is intentional - a deliberate exercise of platform authority. However, both share the same root architectural cause: centralised control. Both require one entity to have sufficient authority over the system to affect all users. Blockchain addresses both by distributing that authority across thousands of independent validators - making both accidental cascade failures and deliberate censorship decisions architecturally impossible at scale. Option D is incorrect: courts can potentially challenge censorship, and technical SPOFs can be mitigated through redundancy.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C

Explanation: The distinction is critical for both understanding the problem and designing solutions. Technical SPOFs (like the AWS December 2021 outage) are unintentional - infrastructure engineers try to prevent them but cannot guarantee zero failures. Governance censorship (like Instagram suppressing a journalist's reach) is intentional - a deliberate exercise of platform authority. However, both share the same root architectural cause: centralised control. Both require one entity to have sufficient authority over the system to affect all users. Blockchain addresses both by distributing that authority across thousands of independent validators - making both accidental cascade failures and deliberate censorship decisions architecturally impossible at scale. Option D is incorrect: courts can potentially challenge censorship, and technical SPOFs can be mitigated through redundancy.

questions.text: In the AWS December 2021 outage, Anjali's entire business (website, payment gateway, inventory system) went offline simultaneously even though each was a separate product from a different company. Why?

options.text: All three products had independent bugs that happened to occur at the same time
All three products were hosted on AWS's us-east-1 region, sharing the same underlying infrastructure SPOF - when that infrastructure failed, all products depending on it failed simultaneously
Anjali's business model was flawed; she should have built all three herself
The products failed because they were all communicating with each other through APIs that went down

questions.explanation: Explanation: This illustrates the hidden danger of infrastructure SPOFs. Anjali correctly chose three separate, reputable products for different business functions - a sensible approach. But all three, despite being different companies' products, ran on the same underlying infrastructure (AWS us-east-1). When that infrastructure failed, the shared SPOF caused all three to fail simultaneously. This is the hidden centralisation of the modern internet: thousands of companies, products, and services all built on three cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) that control ~70% of global cloud infrastructure. "Diversifying" products without diversifying the underlying infrastructure provides false resilience. The correct mitigation: choose products hosted on different cloud providers, different geographic regions - or use blockchain-based alternatives that run on independently-owned, globally-distributed validator infrastructure.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B

Explanation: This illustrates the hidden danger of infrastructure SPOFs. Anjali correctly chose three separate, reputable products for different business functions - a sensible approach. But all three, despite being different companies' products, ran on the same underlying infrastructure (AWS us-east-1). When that infrastructure failed, the shared SPOF caused all three to fail simultaneously. This is the hidden centralisation of the modern internet: thousands of companies, products, and services all built on three cloud providers (AWS, Google Cloud, Azure) that control ~70% of global cloud infrastructure. "Diversifying" products without diversifying the underlying infrastructure provides false resilience. The correct mitigation: choose products hosted on different cloud providers, different geographic regions - or use blockchain-based alternatives that run on independently-owned, globally-distributed validator infrastructure.

questions.text: Which of the following are examples of "governance censorship" (deliberate access restriction) rather than "technical SPOF" (infrastructure failure)? Select ALL that apply.

options.text: Instagram reducing Karan's post reach by 94% after he published political commentary - without notifying him or citing a specific violation
AWS us-east-1 network control plane failing due to an automated management script executing an incorrect command
YouTube demonetising an Indian journalism channel's videos without specifying which videos violated which guidelines
The Indian government ordering ISPs to shut down internet connectivity in a district during civil unrest under Section 144
PhonePe's payment processing failing because Yes Bank's systems were frozen under RBI's moratorium
Amazon Web Services terminating Parler's hosting contract within 24 hours based on content moderation concerns

questions.explanation: Explanation:A - Governance Censorship ✅: Instagram's algorithmic suppression was a deliberate policy decision (whether automated or manual), not a technical failure. The system worked as designed - it reduced distribution of flagged content. A decision, not an accident.B - Technical SPOF ✗: This was an accidental infrastructure failure caused by a bug in AWS's automated management system. Not deliberate. No human decided to take down the network.C - Governance Censorship ✅: YouTube's demonetisation is a deliberate platform policy decision applied algorithmically. The content is still live; the decision to remove monetisation is a governance choice, not a technical failure.D - Governance Censorship ✅: Government-ordered internet shutdowns are explicit governance decisions - legal orders directing ISPs to restrict access. Highly deliberate. India has executed 100+ such shutdowns annually.E - Complex (Neither pure category) ✗: Yes Bank's failure was a financial/regulatory cascade - the RBI moratorium was a governance decision, but its effect on PhonePe was a technical dependency cascade, not deliberate censorship of PhonePe specifically. This is a regulatory chokepoint - a third category.F - Governance Censorship ✅: AWS's termination of Parler was a deliberate business/governance decision by AWS leadership - not a technical failure. AWS infrastructure was functional; the decision to not host Parler was intentional.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answers: A, C, D, F

Explanation:

A - Governance Censorship ✅: Instagram's algorithmic suppression was a deliberate policy decision (whether automated or manual), not a technical failure. The system worked as designed - it reduced distribution of flagged content. A decision, not an accident.

B - Technical SPOF ✗: This was an accidental infrastructure failure caused by a bug in AWS's automated management system. Not deliberate. No human decided to take down the network.

C - Governance Censorship ✅: YouTube's demonetisation is a deliberate platform policy decision applied algorithmically. The content is still live; the decision to remove monetisation is a governance choice, not a technical failure.

D - Governance Censorship ✅: Government-ordered internet shutdowns are explicit governance decisions - legal orders directing ISPs to restrict access. Highly deliberate. India has executed 100+ such shutdowns annually.

E - Complex (Neither pure category) ✗: Yes Bank's failure was a financial/regulatory cascade - the RBI moratorium was a governance decision, but its effect on PhonePe was a technical dependency cascade, not deliberate censorship of PhonePe specifically. This is a regulatory chokepoint - a third category.

F - Governance Censorship ✅: AWS's termination of Parler was a deliberate business/governance decision by AWS leadership - not a technical failure. AWS infrastructure was functional; the decision to not host Parler was intentional.

4/4 marks: All four correct (A, C, D, F) selected, no incorrect ones included
2/4 marks: Any two correct answers selected without including incorrect options

questions.text: Which of the following architectural features of MST Blockchain directly address SPOFs or censorship vulnerabilities? Select ALL that apply and be prepared to explain each.

options.text: 71,000+ independently-owned and operated validators globally - eliminating the infrastructure SPOF by ensuring no single entity controls majority of the network
Smart contract rules for platform operations - replacing company policy decisions with immutable code that requires consensus to change
EVM compatibility - allows Ethereum developers to deploy their code on MST without rewriting it
SARAL Protocol for decentralised identity - creating user identities that no platform can suspend, because they are controlled by the user's own cryptographic key
IPFS integration for content storage - storing content with hash-based addressing so it cannot be removed by any single entity as long as one node globally hosts it
3-second block time - ensuring fast transaction confirmation for user experience parity with centralised apps

questions.explanation: Explanation:A ✅ - Infrastructure SPOF Mitigation: 71,000+ independent validators eliminate the centralised infrastructure SPOF. No single node failure cascades to the network. Even losing 30% of validators (23,000+) leaves the network functional.B ✅ - Censorship Mitigation: Smart contracts replace company policy with code. Platform rules for content distribution, account management, and monetisation are written in publicly auditable code - no company can change rules unilaterally. Any change requires a community governance vote.C ✗ - Performance Feature, Not SPOF/Censorship Mitigation: EVM compatibility is a developer convenience feature. It does not address SPOFs or censorship - it just makes it easier to port existing code.D ✅ - Governance Censorship Mitigation: SARAL-created DIDs cannot be suspended by any platform because the private key is held by the user. No company's database entry controls the identity. Account suspension as a censorship mechanism is architecturally eliminated.E ✅ - Content Censorship Mitigation: IPFS content addressing means content cannot be taken down by targeting a server or an account. The content hash remains valid as long as any IPFS node globally hosts the file - giving global censorship resistance.F ✗ - User Experience Feature: Fast block time improves UX but does not directly address SPOFs or censorship. A censored blockchain that confirms transactions in 3 seconds is still censored.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answers: A, B, D, E

Explanation:

A ✅ - Infrastructure SPOF Mitigation: 71,000+ independent validators eliminate the centralised infrastructure SPOF. No single node failure cascades to the network. Even losing 30% of validators (23,000+) leaves the network functional.

B ✅ - Censorship Mitigation: Smart contracts replace company policy with code. Platform rules for content distribution, account management, and monetisation are written in publicly auditable code - no company can change rules unilaterally. Any change requires a community governance vote.

C ✗ - Performance Feature, Not SPOF/Censorship Mitigation: EVM compatibility is a developer convenience feature. It does not address SPOFs or censorship - it just makes it easier to port existing code.

D ✅ - Governance Censorship Mitigation: SARAL-created DIDs cannot be suspended by any platform because the private key is held by the user. No company's database entry controls the identity. Account suspension as a censorship mechanism is architecturally eliminated.

E ✅ - Content Censorship Mitigation: IPFS content addressing means content cannot be taken down by targeting a server or an account. The content hash remains valid as long as any IPFS node globally hosts the file - giving global censorship resistance.

F ✗ - User Experience Feature: Fast block time improves UX but does not directly address SPOFs or censorship. A censored blockchain that confirms transactions in 3 seconds is still censored.

4/4 marks: All four correct (A, B, D, E) selected with no incorrect options included
2/4 marks: Any two correct answers selected without including incorrect options

questions.text: Ananya is launching a live cricket commentary platform - "CricketVoice" - where 500 independent commentators can stream live audio commentary on cricket matches and earn income from listener subscriptions. The platform will use: AWS for server hosting, YouTube for video backup streams, Razorpay for subscription payments, and Instagram for community building. Write a complete risk analysis identifying: (1) all SPOFs, (2) all censorship vectors, (3) quantified impact of each, and (4) specific MST Blockchain mitigations for each risk.
